artifacts.content:
Hi Mags,

Kids are finally down and James is on his laptop in the “home office” (aka the far corner of the dining room), so I’m grabbing ten quiet minutes to actually write.

I’m still rattled from the West Hills mess. I can’t believe one of the painters Mark lined up was arrested for stealing prescription meds out of the client’s bathroom cabinet. Early March already feels like a year ago, but their attorney from Miller Nash in downtown has been very clear they may file a civil suit and try to name Lumen & Loft directly. I spent half of last Friday on Zoom, walking through photos of the bathroom and our subcontractor agreements instead of looking at fabrics.

Layer that on top of James’s wrongful-termination saga and my brain is fried. He’s been in this dispute with his old fintech employer since March 2024, and things are ramping up again with Miller & Hart ahead of the September hearing. We’re still on that $1,200 monthly retainer, and he keeps asking if I can help organize all the documents from the Sellwood “office” so nothing gets lost.

Meanwhile I’m trying to focus on the website and normal clients. I had a consult in Beaverton last week that reminded me of that April 2024 couple who were three months behind on their mortgage but still talking about a $120k whole-home refresh. I keep thinking I need a better way to flag “handle gently” situations in my notes so I don’t blurt out a huge scope.

Tell me something fun about the kids or Seattle or literally anything that isn’t lawsuits, painters, or budgets.

Love,
Claire

metadata.subject: Catching you up (and unloading a bit)

artifacts.content:
Personal planning – website + life

1. Discreet client care + privacy
- Remember Lake Oswego project with Rachel (wrapped Feb 2023). She was in the middle of a quiet separation and specifically asked that we only use her first name and not tie any images of the kids’ bedrooms to her last name or address. Need an internal “first-name only / no-photo” flag in our case study notes so I don’t accidentally hand something to the web copywriter that exposes too much.
- Same lesson from Anna’s Southeast Portland project (Nov 2022). After she left her abusive partner and moved, she emailed from a new address asking us to take down all images that showed the front of her house and the kids’ playroom, and to confirm in writing that everything was deleted from the site and backups within 14 days. I want a simple internal log for these removals so I can sleep at night.

2. Sensitive family projects
- Mom’s chemo recovery room redo at Sacred Heart in Eugene (started Nov 2024 after Dr. Patel confirmed her Stage II breast cancer). Keep that on a password-protected page, strictly for family.
- Maggie’s Seattle condo / custody-neutral redesign post–King County parenting plan (Jan 2025). Same idea: private login only, no public photos of the kids’ rooms.

3. Messaging
- Public site should just say “We take client privacy seriously” and leave it at that.
- Internal systems carry the real stories.

All-day block: “Family commitments – no new projects”

Notes:
Every year, I need to protect the last week of May and the first week of June. Since Evie’s ADHD and sensory processing diagnoses were finalized in May 2023, those two weeks are stacked with IEP meetings at Llewellyn Elementary and occupational therapy reviews at the Children’s Therapy Clinic on SE Belmont. I don’t want to get into the weeds with clients about diagnoses, but I absolutely can’t be squeezing in new project kickoffs or site visits then.

This year:
- May 30, 2025 – 9:00–11:00 a.m.: Evie IEP review @ Llewellyn Elementary. Bring last quarter’s behavior tracking notes from the app we’ve been using with the school counselor since Sept 2023 for her anxiety/school refusal episodes.
- June 3, 2025 – 2:00 p.m.: OT progress meeting @ Children’s Therapy Clinic (SE Belmont). Ask about adjusting the home sensory setup.

Also need to cross-check these with Dad’s chemo schedule at OHSU. Since July 2024 I’ve been driving him to every other Thursday 8:30–11:00 a.m. chemo block at Oregon Health & Science University Hospital. Add travel buffer so Calendly doesn’t let anyone book consults too close.

Action items before April:
- Update “Work With Us” page copy to say limited availability late May/early June due to recurring family commitments.
- Double-check Google Calendar + Calendly integration so those times are fully blocked from discovery calls and on-site consults.

Hey Dad,

Just got home from the studio and wanted to jot this down before I forget.

First, about Mom: once Dr. Patel confirmed the Stage II breast cancer diagnosis back in November, it felt like the only thing I could actually control was her space. I’ve started sketching ideas for her chemo recovery room at Sacred Heart in Eugene – softer lighting, a small gallery wall with family photos, maybe a narrow shelf for books and tea. If she’s still okay with it, I’d like to document the before-and-after and little progress updates on a private, password-only page on my site so you, Maggie, and the kids can peek in without us putting anything on social media.

On the Portland side, I’m trying to juggle that with driving you to OHSU every other Thursday morning. Since your chemo started in July 2024 at Oregon Health & Science University, I’ve been blocking off 8:30–11:00 a.m. on my work calendar so clients can’t book over your appointments. I’m even asking the web developer if we can make the embedded scheduler respect those recurring blocks automatically.

Work is…a lot. We’re still catching up from when James got laid off from the fintech startup in August 2024 and we fell two months behind on the mortgage with OnPoint. I’ve been quietly doing a couple of sliding-scale projects for parents from Evie’s class at Llewellyn just to keep cash flowing. It’s not ideal, but it’s buying us some breathing room while he and Miller & Hart keep wrestling with the wrongful-termination thing.

We’ll see you next Thursday for your infusion. I’ll bring better snacks this time.

Love,
Claire

Money + clients – brain dump (do not share)

1. Home finances
- James’s layoff from the fintech startup in August 2024 really knocked us sideways. Being two months behind on the OnPoint Community Credit Union mortgage by October was terrifying. We’ve mostly caught up, but it’s fragile.
- To keep cash moving, I’ve been quietly offering sliding-scale or deferred payment options to a few people, especially the Llewellyn Elementary parents who come through Evie’s class. Some bartering (design for cash or services), some discounted retainers.

2. High-risk clients
- Beaverton couple from April 2024: three months behind on their mortgage but wanting a $120,000 whole-home refresh. Need a clear internal-only field in HubSpot to note “financially stretched; propose scaled-down package + extended payment plan.”
- Lake Oswego client with the $3,200 outstanding balance. They’ve already been late twice in the last six months and even threatened to dispute charges with their bank. Right now I’m running their payments through the online portal James set up on his company’s beta platform back in March 2024 so we have clearer tracking than Studio Designer alone.

3. Tax + reporting worries
- That shared Google Sheet James and I created in Jan 2024 to track who’s paying cash or via Zelle so we “don’t have to report more than $10,000 in a single year” is making me queasy. I get why we did it, but I don’t want to build a whole system around “off the books” anything.

4. Website wish
- Internal-only flags for all of the above so I can be ethical, generous, and still realistic about risk.

Hi [Dev Team],

Sharing some context so you understand why I’m being a little intense about privacy, scheduling, and integrations.

On the safety side: in November 2022, one of our Southeast Portland clients, Anna, wrote from a new email after leaving an abusive partner and asked us to remove every image that showed the front of her house and her kids’ playroom. She also wanted confirmation that the photos were deleted from the site and from backups within 14 days. That experience is why I’m asking for an internal log whenever we fully delete a client’s data.

We’ve also had harassment issues. Back in November 2021, a former contractor started texting me gross comments about my Instagram stories. After I filed a complaint, James set up a separate Google Voice number and a logging spreadsheet so any DMs or inquiries routed from Instagram and Pinterest are captured with IPs and timestamps. I’d like the new site to route social leads through that system instead of my cell.

On the more “normal” side of privacy, I have clients like Rachel in Lake Oswego (Feb 2023 project) who are going through quiet separations and only want first-name references, especially when kids’ rooms are involved. I’d love an internal “no-photo / first-name only” toggle in the CMS for case studies.

All of this is layered on top of the usual chaos: managing Evie’s behavior tracking app with Llewellyn since Sept 2023, Dad’s chemo at OHSU every other Thursday, and the wrongful-termination docs James is sharing with Miller & Hart from our Sellwood home office.

Happy to hop on a call to clarify, just didn’t want this all to sound abstract.

Best,
Claire

metadata.subject: Why I’m so picky about privacy + logs

artifacts.content:
James: How’s your day going? Kids alive?

Claire: Barely. Luke dumped yogurt on the rug and Evie had another mini meltdown about math homework.

James: :( Anything urgent on the work front?

Claire: Just the usual circus. Spent the morning trying to draft a calm email to that West Hills client’s attorney at Miller Nash about the painter who stole their prescription meds. I still can’t believe Mark’s guy did that in their bathroom cabinet. Early March feels like a blur of police calls and PR panic.

James: Ugh. Any more word on whether they’ll actually file?

Claire: Their lawyer keeps hinting they might name Lumen & Loft in a civil suit, so I’m talking to our insurance rep tomorrow. On top of that I had to resend the $3,200 invoice to the Lake Oswego client through your fintech beta portal again because they’re late. Twice in six months and now they’re threatening to dispute with their bank.

James: I’ll double-check the logs on the portal tonight.

Claire: Thank you. Also got a new high-budget inquiry through the site – I added them to that January 2024 Google Sheet you made for tracking who might want to pay via cash/Zelle so we don’t cross the $10k/year reporting line. Not sure how I feel about that anymore, but one crisis at a time.

James: We’ll revisit that. Focus on surviving this week.

Claire: Working on it. Are you home in time for Evie’s bedtime story so I can finish this mood board?
